tech.badge: micro:bit + AI · Sensors and pattern analysis
tech.title: Data Detectives: Sensors and AI in Our School Environment
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Columbus City Schools students in Columbus.
content_objectives: Collect environmental data using digital sensors across multiple campus locations
Represent collected data in tables and graphs to support comparison
Identify patterns in physical environment data over time
ai_literacy_objectives: Compare AI-identified patterns with student observations to identify divergences
Apply structured verification practice to evaluate AI output
Articulate criteria for when AI analysis is and is not worth trusting in a multilingual school context
materials: micro:bit with temperature, light, and sound sensors (included in kit)
USB cables and student devices with internet access
AI pattern-analysis tool access (at landing page)
Data recording sheet and chart paper

procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ deploy micro:bit sensors at chosen building locations (cafeteria, library, hallway, courtyard). Record temperature, light, and sound at 5-minute intervals for 25 minutes. Note observations alongside numerical readings.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams input their data into the AI pattern-analysis tool and ask it to identify patterns. Document at least three places the AI agrees with their observations and three where it diverges.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Each team applies the verification protocol to the divergences. Class develops shared trust criteria for AI pattern analysis on data from a campus serving 95 languages and 104 countries.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Trust criteria reference at least one specific divergence the team observed; observations and AI output are documented side by side.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Columbus City Schools)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Data Detectives: Using Sensors and AI to Understand Our School Environment" (grades 6-8, science/math integration). Students collect temperature, light, and sound data with micro:bit sensors across the school building, then use an AI tool to identify patterns. The lesson includes embedded AI literacy objectives: students apply the verification protocol to evaluate whether the AI's pattern analysis matches what they observed firsthand. Tailored to Columbus's Portrait of a Graduate attributes (Technology, Critical Thinking, Adaptability). This is one example of what a teacher produces in a single day of working with us — co-designed with AI, grounded in 15 years of research in STEM integration.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, the sensor data and the AI pattern analysis would land as the answer. After: students treat AI as a teammate to challenge, and the 95-language student body brings community knowledge the model cannot access.

unplugged.badge: No screens · AI prediction critique
unplugged.title: The Prediction Game: Can You Out-Think the Algorithm?
unplugged.overview: Teams receive printed cards with Columbus neighborhood data (demographics, transit, weather). They role-play as an AI system making predictions, then compare with actual outcomes. Students discover what local knowledge adds that data alone misses. Tailored to Columbus's 104 countries and 95 languages — students bring community knowledge the model does not have.
content_objectives: Read and interpret community demographic, transit, and weather data
Generate predictions from data and evaluate them against actual outcomes
Identify gaps between aggregate data and on-the-ground knowledge
ai_literacy_objectives: Distinguish between data an AI has and lived knowledge it does not
Articulate the role of community knowledge in AI prediction quality
Identify specific community inputs no training set captures
materials: Printed Columbus neighborhood data cards (demographics, transit, weather) — PDF at landing page

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams receive printed cards with Columbus neighborhood data. Each team reads the data and writes one prediction per card about what an outcome (transit ridership, weather impact, service demand) might be.

procedure.title: Role-play
procedure.description: Teams role-play as an AI system: using only the data on the cards, generate a "prediction." Then receive the actual outcome packet. Document where the prediction held and where it failed.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Teams identify what local knowledge — speaking 95 languages, having family in 104 countries — adds that the data alone misses. Class builds a shared list of community-knowledge inputs no AI training set contains.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final list cites at least three specific community-knowledge inputs and ties each to a divergence the team observed.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Columbus City Schools)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: The Prediction Game: Can You Out-Think the Algorithm? — Teams receive printed cards with Columbus neighborhood data (demographics, transit, weather). They role-play as an AI system making predictions, then compare with actual outcomes. Students discover what local knowledge adds that data alone misses. Tailored to Columbus's 104 countries and 95 languages — students bring community knowledge the model does not have.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, an AI prediction about Columbus neighborhoods would be a fact to discuss. After: students role-play as the AI itself, then catch what 104 countries of community knowledge add — knowledge no training set contains.

43207-ccs.policyLandscape: Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model AI policy available; most districts will adopt or adapt; operationalization is the real work
43207-ccs.districtNeeds: Most Ohio districts will adopt the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model policy or a close variant — the governance piece is straightforward. The harder, more important work is operationalizing it: what does teaching and learning actually look like when AI tools are available to students and educators? The model policy calls for 'AI literacy' and 'clearly articulated expectations for acceptable student uses,' but translating that language into classroom practice requires structured time for teachers to explore, build, and develop their own pedagogical approaches. That is the work Freyja Labs supports — grounded in the CRAFT framework (Contextualize, Reframe, Assemble, Fortify, Transfer), which gives educators a repeatable process for designing instruction that holds up as tools and contexts change.
43207-ccs.contextNarrative: Columbus City Schools is in the early years of Dr. Chapman's tenure as CEO, navigating a "Power of One" strategic plan and a Portrait of a Graduate built around Technology, Critical Thinking, and Adaptability. Across 113 schools, students bring 95 languages and roots in 104 countries — the kind of diversity that makes one-size-fits-all PD obviously wrong. With the Wallace investment supporting the community campus model and Ohio's forthcoming AI policy guidance giving every district a starting point, the harder work is what comes after the policy is adopted: what teaching actually looks like when AI is in the room. That's where Freyja Labs comes in: we custom-build a path with your teachers, designed for what your students need and what your community is already navigating.

44114-cmsd.connectionPoint: Building Brighter Futures consolidation (29 schools merging, adopted Dec 2025). 100% of buildings will be new or upgraded. Expanding career pathways and algebra access at every K-8. Dr. Morgan's first major restructuring.

procedure.title: Plan
procedure.description: Teams receive printed enrollment, condition, and community-context data for fictional schools. Each team designs a consolidation plan using only the printed data and their own reasoning.

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Cleveland Metropolitan SD)

Unplugged debate: When should data override community voice?

44114-cmsd.policyLandscape: Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model AI policy available; most districts will adopt or adapt; operationalization is the real work
44114-cmsd.districtNeeds: Most Ohio districts will adopt the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model policy or a close variant — the governance piece is straightforward. The harder, more important work is operationalizing it: what does teaching and learning actually look like when AI tools are available to students and educators? The model policy calls for 'AI literacy' and 'clearly articulated expectations for acceptable student uses,' but translating that language into classroom practice requires structured time for teachers to explore, build, and develop their own pedagogical approaches. That is the work Freyja Labs supports — grounded in the CRAFT framework (Contextualize, Reframe, Assemble, Fortify, Transfer), which gives educators a repeatable process for designing instruction that holds up as tools and contexts change.
44114-cmsd.contextNarrative: Cleveland Metropolitan is in the middle of its biggest restructuring in years — the Building Brighter Futures consolidation merges 29 schools, with 100% of buildings new or upgraded and career pathways and algebra access expanding at every K-8. It is Dr. Morgan's first major restructuring as superintendent, which means everything is in motion at once. Ohio's model AI policy will give the district governance language; what it won't give is the classroom-level practice that makes the policy real. We design with your teachers, not for them — translating policy language into something they can actually do on a Tuesday in October, in the context of the consolidation your community is living through.

45219-cps.connectionPoint: Issue 28 passed — $48M/year for 10 years. Supt Murphy's "Here for Kids" motto and Three Es (employed, enlisted, enrolled). CTE pathways at every high school. Already adopted AI policy Sept 2024 — a year early.
45219-cps.standardsFramework: Ohio Learning Standards (OLS). CS: Ohio CS Model Curriculum. Science: Ohio's Learning Standards for Science. Math: Ohio's Learning Standards for Mathematics. AI literacy objectives: align to Ohio AI in Education Model Policy framework.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: Career Data Lab: AI and Workforce Trends
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Cincinnati Public Schools students in Cincinnati.
content_objectives: Pull and interpret current Cincinnati-area workforce data from authoritative sources
Compare two information sources (AI and BLS) on the same career questions
Construct evidence-based positions on the use of AI for career guidance
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify divergences between AI-generated career projections and BLS data
Apply structured verification practice to AI workforce claims
Articulate criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI career recommendations
materials: Bureau of Labor Statistics data set for the Cincinnati MSA (printed and digital, at landing page)
AI tool access for workforce-projection queries
Three Es career-pathway prompt cards (employed, enlisted, enrolled)

procedure.title: Pull
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ select 3–5 careers spanning the Three Es (employed, enlisted, enrolled). Query an AI tool for current and projected workforce trends. Record AI claims with timestamps and exact prompts.

procedure.title: Verify
procedure.description: Teams cross-reference each AI claim against BLS data for the Cincinnati MSA. Document where AI matches BLS, where it diverges, and where AI introduces context not in the underlying data.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to AI workforce projections. Each team makes a case for or against using AI for student career guidance. Class builds a shared protocol for when AI workforce advice is worth trusting.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Each team's verdict cites at least three specific AI/BLS divergences and pairs each with a recommendation about how to use (or not use) AI in counseling practice.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Cincinnati Public Schools)

43123-swcs.standardsFramework: Ohio Learning Standards (OLS). CS: Ohio CS Model Curriculum. Science: Ohio's Learning Standards for Science. Math: Ohio's Learning Standards for Mathematics. AI literacy objectives: align to Ohio AI in Education Model Policy framework.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.badge: micro:bit + AI · Growth and change measurement
tech.title: Growth Patterns: Measuring Change in a Growing Community
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for South-Western City SD students in Grove City.
content_objectives: Measure environmental and motion data over time
Make and verify predictions using empirical data
Identify how rapidly conditions can change in a growing community
ai_literacy_objectives: Compare AI predictions against real-time measurements
Apply structured verification practice to predictive AI output
Articulate when an AI model lags behind community change
materials: micro:bit with temperature, light, and motion sensors (included in kit)
Data recording sheet and chart paper

procedure.title: Measure
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ collect environmental and motion data at locations on campus (busy hallway, quiet courtyard, classroom transition). Record at 5-minute intervals; note what is changing in real time.

procedure.title: Predict
procedure.description: Teams input early data into the AI tool and ask it to predict how the rest of the day or week will unfold. Document divergences as they occur in real time.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to AI predictions about a fast-changing community. Each team produces a verdict: when does change move faster than the model predicting it?

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verdict cites at least one prediction and one observed outcome that diverged, with reasoning grounded in the data.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with South-Western City SD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Growth Patterns: Measuring Change in a Growing Community" (grades 4-6, math/science). Students use micro:bit sensors to collect data, then use AI to identify patterns and make predictions. Embedded AI literacy: students compare AI predictions with their own reasoning. Tailored to South-Western's identity as a fast-growing suburban district.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI predictions about a growing community would be the forecast. After: students collect their own sensor data, compare with AI predictions, and learn that change moves faster than the model that predicts it.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Population-growth prediction
unplugged.title: New Neighbors: How Do Communities Change?
unplugged.overview: Teams analyze printed population-growth data for a suburban community and predict what services will be needed. They compare their predictions with AI-generated projections and identify what the model misses about community character. Tailored to South-Western's rapid growth.
content_objectives: Interpret population-growth data and project future service needs
Compare two projection approaches on the same data
Identify factors that quantitative models miss about community
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify what AI population projections capture and what they miss
Distinguish between numerical projections and community-character forecasting
Articulate when human judgment is essential to community planning
materials: Printed population-growth data for a fictional fast-growing suburb (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Predict
procedure.description: Teams receive printed population-growth data and predict what services (schools, transit, parks) will be needed in 3, 5, and 10 years using only the data and reasoning.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams receive the AI-generated service-needs projection for the same data. Document where AI matches their predictions and where it diverges.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Teams identify what an AI projection misses about community character — how the new families and existing families build a place. Each team produces a recommendation for what should and should not be left to AI.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final recommendation cites specific divergences between team and AI projections plus at least one community-character factor the model cannot represent.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with South-Western City SD)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: New Neighbors: How Do Communities Change? — Teams analyze printed population-growth data for a suburban community and predict what services will be needed. They compare their predictions with AI-generated projections and identify what the model misses about community character. Tailored to South-Western's rapid growth.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, an AI population projection would be the plan. After: students predict community needs using only what they observe locally, then identify exactly what the model misses about a fast-growing suburban district.

43123-swcs.policyLandscape: Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model AI policy available; most districts will adopt or adapt; operationalization is the real work
43123-swcs.districtNeeds: Most Ohio districts will adopt the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model policy or a close variant — the governance piece is straightforward. The harder, more important work is operationalizing it: what does teaching and learning actually look like when AI tools are available to students and educators? The model policy calls for 'AI literacy' and 'clearly articulated expectations for acceptable student uses,' but translating that language into classroom practice requires structured time for teachers to explore, build, and develop their own pedagogical approaches. That is the work Freyja Labs supports — grounded in the CRAFT framework (Contextualize, Reframe, Assemble, Fortify, Transfer), which gives educators a repeatable process for designing instruction that holds up as tools and contexts change.
43123-swcs.contextNarrative: South-Western City is the fastest-growing suburban district in central Ohio, with new families arriving in Grove City and Galloway and new teachers joining each year. That growth is its own kind of opportunity: a shared foundation-building moment when everyone is, in some sense, new. Ohio's model AI policy is on the way, and the district will adopt or adapt some version of it. The question isn't governance — it's what teaching looks like when AI is in the room and a third of the staff is in their first three years. We custom-build a shared design practice with your staff — durable rather than improvised, made for the foundation you're laying as the district grows.

43035-olsd.connectionPoint: Top-performing suburban district in Delaware County. Columbus tech corridor families. High STEM/AP participation already — expectation is forward-looking preparation.

tech.title: AI in the Real World: Intelligent Systems in Our Community
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Olentangy Local SD students in Lewis Center.
content_objectives: Connect classroom data investigation to real-world industry applications
Design and test a simple classification rule
Compare classification approaches on shared data
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify how AI classification systems work in industries familiar to the community
Apply structured verification practice to industry AI decisions
Articulate trade-offs between rule-based and learned classification
materials: AI tool access (chosen by each team)
Industry/career application cards (Columbus tech corridor sectors)
Data recording sheet and chart paper

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams choose an industry along the Columbus tech corridor (insurance, healthcare, logistics, finance) and identify a real AI application in that field. Use micro:bit data to simulate a small classification problem the industry might face.

procedure.title: Build
procedure.description: Each team builds a simple classification rule using their sensor data (e.g., "if temperature > X and motion = Y, classify as Z"). Test the rule against new data; record errors.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to AI decision-making in their chosen industry context. Compare a rule-based human classifier with an AI classifier — which is more transparent? Which is more accurate? Which would families in tech-corridor jobs trust?

check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

unplugged.badge: No screens · AI hiring fairness debate
unplugged.title: The Hiring Committee: AI vs. Human Judgment
unplugged.overview: Teams review fictional job applications (printed) and rank candidates. They then see how an AI screening tool ranked the same candidates — and debate which approach produced fairer results. Designed for a community where many parents work in tech hiring.
content_objectives: Apply a shared rubric to evaluate qualifications
Compare two ranking approaches and analyze the differences
Construct an evidence-based argument about decision-making fairness
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify the data inputs an AI hiring tool uses and the assumptions encoded
Distinguish between rule-based and pattern-based ranking decisions
Articulate when human judgment should override AI ranking in hiring
materials: Printed fictional job-application packets (4–6 candidates) — PDF at landing page

procedure.title: Rank
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive printed fictional job applications and rank candidates using a shared rubric. Document the reasoning behind their ranking.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams receive the AI screening tool's ranking for the same candidates. Document where rankings agree and where they diverge.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Structured debate: which approach produced fairer results? Each team takes a position grounded in evidence from the packets. Frame around the reality that many Olentangy parents make hiring decisions professionally.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Argument cites specific candidates, specific rubric criteria, and specific divergences between team and AI rankings.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Olentangy Local SD)

unplugged.district_framing: The Hiring Committee: AI vs. Human Judgment — Teams review fictional job applications (printed) and rank candidates. They then see how an AI screening tool ranked the same candidates — and debate which approach produced fairer results. Designed for a community where many parents work in tech hiring.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, an AI hiring tool ranking would feel impartial. After: students rank the same fictional candidates, surface their own assumptions, and debate which approach actually produced fairer results — a question many of their parents face professionally.

43035-olsd.policyLandscape: Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model AI policy available; most districts will adopt or adapt; operationalization is the real work
43035-olsd.districtNeeds: Most Ohio districts will adopt the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model policy or a close variant — the governance piece is straightforward. The harder, more important work is operationalizing it: what does teaching and learning actually look like when AI tools are available to students and educators? The model policy calls for 'AI literacy' and 'clearly articulated expectations for acceptable student uses,' but translating that language into classroom practice requires structured time for teachers to explore, build, and develop their own pedagogical approaches. That is the work Freyja Labs supports — grounded in the CRAFT framework (Contextualize, Reframe, Assemble, Fortify, Transfer), which gives educators a repeatable process for designing instruction that holds up as tools and contexts change.
43035-olsd.contextNarrative: Olentangy is the top-performing suburban district in Delaware County, drawing families from the Columbus tech corridor whose expectations for the schools mirror what they expect at work: forward-looking preparation, not catch-up. STEM and AP participation is already high — what's missing is a structured way for teachers to bring AI literacy into their existing strong instruction without making it feel like one more initiative. Ohio's policy guidance will arrive in 2026; what teachers need before then is a pedagogical framework that lets them design lessons where students engage AI critically as part of rigorous learning, not alongside it. We design that depth with your teachers, custom-built for Olentangy students and the community's expectations.

43017-dcsd.connectionPoint: Globally connected suburban community. Strong AP/STEM track record. Many families in tech and research sectors.
