tech.title: Consistent Systems: Reliable Data Across Classrooms
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Cypress-Fairbanks ISD students in Cypress.
content_objectives: Design a reproducible measurement protocol
Execute another team's protocol and evaluate its clarity
Compare data consistency across teams and environments
ai_literacy_objectives: Distinguish between protocol-driven reliability and AI-output reliability
Apply structured verification practice to questions of consistency
Articulate what "reliable" means when humans and AI are both in the system
materials: micro:bit with sensors (included in kit) — multiple per team for parallel measurement
Data recording sheet across multiple classroom environments

procedure.title: Design
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ design a data collection protocol intended to produce comparable results across different classroom environments. Document the protocol step by step.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams swap protocols with another team and execute the other team's protocol in a new classroom. Run AI analysis on both teams' data to compare consistency.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to the question of reliability — both human protocol and AI analysis. Class produces a shared definition of "reliable" when both humans and AI are part of the system.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final definition of reliability cites both a protocol decision and an AI behavior that affected consistency, with evidence from team data.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Cypress-Fairbanks ISD)

unplugged.badge: No screens · When averages lie
unplugged.title: The School Comparison: When Averages Lie
unplugged.overview: Teams receive printed performance data for fictional schools across a large district. They calculate averages, then examine what the averages hide about individual students and schools. Connected to Cy-Fair's challenge of coherence across 90+ campuses.
content_objectives: Calculate and visualize statistical averages
Identify what averages obscure about underlying data
Construct an evidence-based argument about appropriate use of summary statistics
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify how AI trained on aggregate statistics can mask within-group differences
Distinguish between describing a population and describing individuals
Articulate when AI averages are appropriate evidence and when they are not
materials: Printed performance data for fictional schools across a large district (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Calculate
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive printed performance data for fictional schools and calculate averages by school and across the district.

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams visualize the underlying distributions on chart paper. Identify what each average hides — students at the tails, schools that look average but contain very different populations.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written critique: when should "the average" be used in district decisions, and when is it dangerous? Connect to the challenge of coherence across 90+ campuses.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Critique cites at least two specific cases where the average misrepresents the data and proposes when averages are appropriate.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Northside ISD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data
Collect, transform, and analyze data using digital tools and explain how the data inform a decision
Use computational tools to collect, transform, analyze, and visualize data to answer a question
Use computational models, including simulations, to investigate phenomena and explain results
Use text evidence to support an appropriate response

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Mission Ready: Using Sensors and AI for Real-World Problem Solving" (grades 7-9, science/CTE). Students design a micro:bit sensor system for a real-world challenge (environmental monitoring, resource tracking), then evaluate AI recommendations. Embedded AI literacy: students assess AI outputs against mission requirements — what matters is whether the answer is good enough to act on. Tailored to San Antonio's military-readiness and healthcare career culture.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, designing a sensor system is an engineering exercise. After: students design for a real-world challenge and evaluate AI recommendations against mission requirements — the action-bias of the surrounding military and healthcare workforce, applied to AI.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Decision under incomplete intelligence
unplugged.title: Mission Briefing: Incomplete Intelligence
unplugged.overview: Teams receive a printed "intelligence briefing" with gaps. They must decide what action to take with incomplete information, then examine how an AI recommendation engine would handle the same gaps. Connected to San Antonio's military decision-making culture.
content_objectives: Make a defensible decision under documented uncertainty
Identify gaps in available information
Compare decisions reached by different reasoning paths
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify when AI fills in missing information with assumptions
Apply structured verification practice to AI decisions made under uncertainty
Articulate when AI assumption-filling is acceptable and when it is dangerous
materials: Printed "intelligence briefing" packets with intentional gaps (PDF at landing page)
AI recommendation packet based on the same incomplete data

procedure.title: Decide
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive a printed briefing with documented gaps. Decide what action to take using only the available information; document the gaps and the reasoning behind the decision.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams examine an AI recommendation generated from the same incomplete data. Identify what assumptions the AI filled in for the missing information.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written defense of their decision and a critique of the AI's. Connect to the everyday adaptability of San Antonio's military and healthcare communities.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Defense names the gaps, the team's decision, the AI's assumptions, and a defensible reason for choosing one over the other.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Northside ISD)

78238-nisd.policyLandscape: CS = LOTE sub; TEA shortage area
78238-nisd.districtNeeds: Texas districts have autonomy in how they approach CS and AI, and the CS teacher-shortage designation signals that demand is outpacing the supply of dedicated specialists. The more sustainable path is building computational thinking capacity across content areas — helping existing teachers develop pedagogical approaches that integrate computing into their practice. As AI tools become more present in classrooms, teachers also need a structured way to help students engage with those tools critically. The CRAFT framework provides that structure, and the Check the Machine protocol gives students a repeatable verification practice they can apply across subjects and tools.
78238-nisd.contextNarrative: Northside is the largest district in the San Antonio metro and the fastest-growing in Texas, with strong military and healthcare career pathways shaping how students think about their futures. AI literacy fits naturally into both — the verification habits we build with your teachers are exactly what military and clinical contexts depend on. With Texas's CS teacher-shortage designation in play, the realistic path is helping teachers in core content areas build computational thinking pedagogy they can actually use. We custom-build practices with your teachers that transfer across the rapidly growing population — from older campuses to buildings still being designed.

77494-kisd.connectionPoint: Energy corridor, Texas Medical Center, aerospace community. STEM-oriented families. High expectations for tech integration.

image.alt: Suburban community and open prairie in Katy

77494-kisd.standardsFramework: Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS). Must include TEKS codes. Also include English Language Proficiency Standards (ELPS) alignment. CS: TEKS Chapter 126. Science: TEKS Chapter 112. Math: TEKS Chapter 111.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: Energy Data: AI and the Systems Around Us
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Katy ISD students in Katy.
content_objectives: Measure proxies for energy use in a real environment
Compare AI model predictions with physical-system observations
Identify where models simplify physical reality
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify oversimplifications in AI models of physical systems
Apply structured verification practice to model-based AI predictions
Articulate the boundary between useful simplification and misleading abstraction
materials: micro:bit with light, temperature, and motion sensors (included in kit)
Data recording sheet and chart paper

procedure.title: Measure
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ measure energy-related data on campus (light, temperature, motion correlated with HVAC and occupancy). Record at 5-minute intervals.

procedure.title: Model
procedure.description: Teams input data into an AI energy-modeling tool and ask it to identify patterns and predict next-hour conditions. Document where the model captures real dynamics and where it oversimplifies.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to AI energy modeling. Class produces a list of physical-system realities that AI energy models commonly miss — relevant to the families working in Houston's energy corridor.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final list cites at least three specific physical realities AI missed and the energy-system implication of each.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

tech.title: Foundations: Understanding That Transfers
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Fort Worth ISD students in Fort Worth.
content_objectives: Build a small, complete data investigation
Apply a verification protocol systematically
Identify what transfers across contexts and what is context-specific
ai_literacy_objectives: Apply the verification protocol step by step
Distinguish between the protocol and the specific tool/topic
Articulate which verification habits travel with the learner
materials: Data recording sheet and chart paper

procedure.title: Build
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ build a small micro:bit data project (their choice of focus) and collect data. The structure of the build matters more than the specific topic — practice in building.

procedure.title: Verify
procedure.description: Teams run AI analysis on the data and apply the verification protocol step by step. Document each step on a verification card.

procedure.title: Transfer
procedure.description: Class shares verification cards. Each team identifies which steps would transfer to a different topic, a different curriculum, a different AI tool — practicing the meta-skill that survives institutional change.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verification card explicitly identifies which steps are tool-specific and which are transferable, with reasoning for each.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Fort Worth ISD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data
Collect, transform, and analyze data using digital tools and explain how the data inform a decision
Use computational tools to collect, transform, analyze, and visualize data to answer a question
Use computational models, including simulations, to investigate phenomena and explain results
Use text evidence to support an appropriate response

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Foundations: Building Understanding That Transfers" (grades 5-7, science/math). Students build a micro:bit data project, then practice a structured verification practice with AI-generated analysis. Embedded AI literacy: skills that travel with the teacher and the student regardless of context. Designed for stability during transition.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, the lesson is one more thing to teach during a transition. After: students learn the verification protocol — a verification habit that travels with them to whatever curriculum, district, or AI tool comes next.

unplugged.badge: No screens · The transfer student record
unplugged.title: The Transfer Student: What Your Record Doesn't Show
unplugged.overview: Teams examine a fictional student's printed academic record and discuss what an AI system would infer vs. what a teacher who knows the student would know. Discussion about what gets lost in data transfers. Relevant during institutional transition.
content_objectives: Read a complex academic record and infer about a student
Compare two inference approaches on the same data
Identify what context cannot be captured in standardized records
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify what AI inference systems capture and what they miss about a person
Distinguish between data points and lived knowledge
Articulate the role of human relationship in evaluating AI inferences
materials: Printed fictional student academic record (PDF at landing page)
AI inference output based on the record

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ examine a fictional student's printed record and predict what an AI placement system would infer about the student.

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams compare predictions with the AI output. Identify what the AI inferred and what it missed about the student's real situation.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written response: what does a teacher who knows the student know that the record cannot show? Frame around the reality of frequent student transfers during institutional change.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Written response names at least three pieces of teacher knowledge that the AI cannot have and ties each to a likely error in the AI inference.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

procedure.title: Evaluate

talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate

Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Austin ISD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data
Collect, transform, and analyze data using digital tools and explain how the data inform a decision
Use computational tools to collect, transform, analyze, and visualize data to answer a question
Use computational models, including simulations, to investigate phenomena and explain results
Use text evidence to support an appropriate response

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Question Everything: Designing Experiments to Test AI Claims" (grades 7-10, science). Students use micro:bit sensors to collect empirical data, then design experiments to test whether AI-generated claims about their data are accurate. Embedded AI literacy: scientific method applied to AI outputs. Tailored to Austin's intellectual culture and strong PD expectations.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, "evaluating AI claims" is a goal. After: students design experiments — actual experiments with controls — that test whether AI-generated claims about their data hold up. Scientific method meets AI evaluation.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Designing the test AI cannot pass
unplugged.title: The Experiment Design Challenge: Can You Design a Test AI Can't Pass?
unplugged.overview: Teams design a hands-on experiment (paper, ruler, tape) that produces results an AI could not predict from prior data alone. Students discover the limits of pattern-matching vs. physical investigation. Designed for Austin's intellectually curious PD culture.
content_objectives: Design a controllable physical experiment
Document the difference between predicted and observed outcomes
Identify question types that require physical investigation
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify the limits of pattern matching as a knowledge tool
Distinguish between predictions from data and predictions from physical principles
Articulate when an AI cannot, in principle, answer a question
materials: Paper, ruler, tape, and other simple physical materials

procedure.title: Design
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ design a hands-on physical experiment that produces a result an AI could not predict from prior data alone. Document the design and the prediction reasoning.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams execute the experiment and record actual results. Compare with what an AI trained on prior similar data would have predicted.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team articulates exactly what their experiment shows about the limits of pattern matching versus physical investigation. Class shares experiments and builds a list of question types AI cannot answer from data alone.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final list names at least three categories of question that require physical investigation and one example from a team's experiment for each.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Austin ISD)

Students discover the limits of pattern-matching vs. physical investigation. Designed for Austin's intellectually curious PD culture.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, talking about AI limits is a discussion. After: students design a hands-on experiment that produces results an AI cannot predict from prior data — the durable proof that pattern-matching is not understanding.

78704-aisd.policyLandscape: CS = LOTE sub; TEA shortage area
78704-aisd.districtNeeds: Texas districts have autonomy in how they approach CS and AI, and the CS teacher-shortage designation signals that demand is outpacing the supply of dedicated specialists. The more sustainable path is building computational thinking capacity across content areas — helping existing teachers develop pedagogical approaches that integrate computing into their practice. As AI tools become more present in classrooms, teachers also need a structured way to help students engage with those tools critically. The CRAFT framework provides that structure, and the Check the Machine protocol gives students a repeatable verification practice they can apply across subjects and tools.
78704-aisd.contextNarrative: Austin ISD has a strong PD culture — teachers seek professional learning voluntarily and the bar for what counts as worth attending is high. That's the right pressure to design under. Workshops that don't leave teachers with materials they actually use don't survive in this environment. Superintendent Segura's leadership and Texas's CS teacher-shortage designation point in the same direction: build computational thinking capacity across content areas through pedagogy that respects what teachers already know. We design with that audience in mind: substance over slogans, materials your teachers continue to use, a practice they can adapt to their specific students.

77479-fbisd.connectionPoint: One of the most ethnically diverse districts in the nation. Sugar Land to Missouri City. 130+ languages.

image.alt: Suburban community in Fort Bend County

77479-fbisd.standardsFramework: Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS). Must include TEKS codes. Also include English Language Proficiency Standards (ELPS) alignment. CS: TEKS Chapter 126. Science: TEKS Chapter 112. Math: TEKS Chapter 111.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.badge: AI literacy + multilingual data · No micro:bit
tech.title: Many Languages, One Method: AI and Diverse Data
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Fort Bend ISD students in Sugar Land.
content_objectives: Compare AI output across multiple languages on identical prompts
Identify cultural and linguistic biases in AI behavior
Document evidence systematically across language conditions
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify English-centric defaults in AI behavior
Apply structured verification practice to multilingual AI evaluation
Articulate AI bias using the student's own multilingual community as the dataset
materials: Student devices with AI tool access
Comparable text/data prompts in multiple languages (community languages of Fort Bend)

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ select 3 community languages and submit identical prompts to an AI tool in each language. Record outputs.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams compare AI performance across the languages. Document where output quality differs, where cultural context is mishandled, and where the AI defaults to an English-centric framing.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Each team produces evidence about how AI handles the languages of the Fort Bend community — the multilingual community itself becomes the dataset.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Evidence names at least one quality difference, one cultural mishandling, and one English-centric default per language tested.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

unplugged.badge: No screens · AI translation across community languages
unplugged.title: The Translation Table: AI Across Cultures
unplugged.overview: Teams test AI translations across languages their families speak. Without screens — using printed AI outputs — students evaluate accuracy and cultural nuance. Designed for Fort Bend's 130+ languages and the community knowledge students bring.
content_objectives: Compare translations of identical text across languages

Construct an evidence-based recommendation for cross-language communication

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive printed AI translations across 3 community languages. Students who speak each language take the lead in evaluating accuracy and cultural fit.

procedure.title: Investigate

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written verdict: when is AI translation appropriate for community communication, and when must a native speaker be involved? Connect to Fort Bend's 130+ languages.

77479-fbisd.districtNeeds: Texas districts have autonomy in how they approach CS and AI, and the CS teacher-shortage designation signals that demand is outpacing the supply of dedicated specialists. The more sustainable path is building computational thinking capacity across content areas — helping existing teachers develop pedagogical approaches that integrate computing into their practice. As AI tools become more present in classrooms, teachers also need a structured way to help students engage with those tools critically. The CRAFT framework provides that structure, and the Check the Machine protocol gives students a repeatable verification practice they can apply across subjects and tools.
77479-fbisd.contextNarrative: Fort Bend ISD is one of the most ethnically diverse districts in the nation, with families speaking 130+ languages across Sugar Land and Missouri City. That diversity is a pedagogical asset for AI literacy: students bring multiple cultural lenses to questions about whose data shapes a model and whose voice is missing from an output. With Texas's CS teacher-shortage designation, content-area integration is the sustainable path. We co-design with your teachers — custom-built for the 130+ languages Fort Bend students bring, where multilingual lived experience becomes the verification source.
