misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Los Angeles Unified)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "My Neighborhood, My Data: What AI Sees and What It Misses" (grades 6-8, science/social studies). Students collect environmental data in their school neighborhood, use AI to analyze it, then evaluate whether AI's conclusions match their community's lived experience. Embedded AI literacy: AI bias evaluated through local knowledge. Designed for a district where every school is a different context.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI environmental analysis lands as the analysis. After: students evaluate whether the AI's conclusions match their community's lived experience, and learn to use local knowledge as the ground truth — exactly the move AB 2876 will require at scale.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Whose neighborhood story is this?
unplugged.title: My Neighborhood's Story: Who Gets to Tell It?
unplugged.overview: Teams examine printed AI-generated neighborhood descriptions for three LA communities. Students evaluate accuracy using their own knowledge and identify what the AI got wrong about places they know. 420K students means no two neighborhoods are the same.
content_objectives: Read AI-generated descriptive text critically
Distinguish factual error, framing error, and absence
Construct an evidence-based written argument
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify whose perspective is encoded in AI neighborhood descriptions
Position lived knowledge as authoritative evidence
Articulate when AI descriptions of communities should and should not ship
materials: Printed AI-generated descriptions of three LA neighborhoods (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive printed AI-generated descriptions of three LA neighborhoods. Annotate where each description rings true and where it feels off.

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Students who know each neighborhood take the lead in identifying what the AI got wrong. Document factual errors, framing errors, and absences (what the AI did not include at all).

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written verdict: should this AI description be shown to a newcomer to LA? Defend with packet evidence and lived knowledge.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verdict cites at least three specific issues per neighborhood (factual, framing, or absence) and a defensible recommendation.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Los Angeles Unified)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: My Neighborhood's Story: Who Gets to Tell It? — Teams examine printed AI-generated neighborhood descriptions for three LA communities. Students evaluate accuracy using their own knowledge and identify what the AI got wrong about places they know. 420K students means no two neighborhoods are the same.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, AI neighborhood descriptions are descriptions. After: students examine printed AI descriptions of LA communities they know and identify what the AI got wrong about the places they live in. 420K students means no two neighborhoods are the same — and the model can't see most of them.

90017-lausd.districtNeeds: California is shaping what AI literacy means across core subjects through AB 2876 — math, science, and history-social science frameworks will all need to reflect AI thinking. For districts, this is a pedagogical design challenge: how do teachers integrate AI literacy into their existing practice without adding a new prep? The answer lies in structured approaches to lesson design that treat AI as a context for critical thinking rather than a tool to master. The CRAFT framework and the Check the Machine verification protocol are built for this — they help teachers design instruction where students learn to question, verify, and own their work when AI is part of the process.
90017-lausd.contextNarrative: LAUSD is the second-largest district in the U.S. — 420,000 students under Superintendent Carvalho — and California's AB 2876 mandates AI literacy framework integration across math, science, and history-social science. At this scale, PD that doesn't adapt to local school context fails by design. The pedagogical question is the same district-wide: how do teachers integrate AI literacy into their existing practice without adding a new prep? We co-design with LAUSD teachers — treating AI as a context for critical thinking, not a tool to master, with practices custom-built for the wildly different schools that make up your district.

92103-sdusd.connectionPoint: Biotech corridor + military (Naval Base SD) + bilingual border community. Applied, practical community identity.
92103-sdusd.standardsFramework: California Content Standards + Next Generation Science Standards (CA-NGSS). CS: CA CS Standards (2018). Math: CA-CCSSM. AI literacy: align to AB 2876 framework integration requirements.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: Border Data: AI Across Communities
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for San Diego Unified students in San Diego.
content_objectives: Compare datasets across two national contexts
Identify reporting and measurement convention differences
Document AI handling of contextual differences
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify single-jurisdiction defaults in AI analysis
Apply structured verification practice to cross-border data
Articulate when AI handling of cross-context data is appropriate
materials: Comparable datasets from US and Mexico contexts (printed)

procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ collect data on campus and pair it with a comparable dataset from a Mexico-side context (provided). Document differences in measurement units, time conventions, and reporting standards.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to analyze the combined dataset. Document where AI handles cross-border data well and where it imposes a single-jurisdiction default.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to AI handling of cross-border data. Class produces evidence about data discontinuities the AI did and did not handle — relevant to a binational community.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Evidence cites at least two specific cross-border discontinuities and pairs each with how AI handled (or mishandled) it.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

tech.title: Sustained Improvement: Data That Gets Better Over Time
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Long Beach USD students in Long Beach.
content_objectives: Track data over multiple days
Distinguish trends from statistical noise
Produce evidence-based judgments about improvement claims
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify when AI-claimed trends are genuine and when they are artifacts
Apply structured verification practice to longitudinal AI claims
Articulate honest evidentiary standards for improvement

procedure.title: Track
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ build a micro:bit data-collection setup that runs for multiple days. Sample data is provided if multi-day collection is not possible in the workshop window.

procedure.title: Analyze
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to identify trends in the multi-day data. Document each AI-claimed trend.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol plus a real-vs-artifact analysis: which AI-identified trends are genuine and which are statistical artifacts? Class produces a Long Beach-quality framework for honest improvement claims.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Framework cites at least one trend judged genuine and one judged artifact, with reasoning grounded in the data.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Long Beach USD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Sustained Improvement: Using Data to Get Better Over Time" (grades 5-7, science/math). Students build a micro:bit data-collection system, track patterns over multiple days, and use AI to identify trends. Embedded AI literacy: evaluating whether AI-identified trends are real or artifacts. Designed for a Broad Prize district that understands sustained improvement.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, an AI-identified trend is a trend. After: students track patterns over multiple days and learn to distinguish genuine improvement from statistical artifact — the same honest assessment the Broad Prize culture is built on.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Real or artifact?
unplugged.title: The Improvement Claim: Real or Artifact?
unplugged.overview: Teams receive printed trend data showing "improvement" and must determine whether the improvement is genuine or a statistical artifact. Students apply the same critical lens to AI-generated trend claims. Connected to Long Beach's culture of honest assessment.
content_objectives: Distinguish trend from artifact in printed data
Identify statistical reasons a trend may be artifactual
Construct an evidence-based evaluation of improvement claims
ai_literacy_objectives: Apply skepticism to AI-generated improvement claims
Identify which AI claims would and would not survive honest review
Articulate honest assessment standards for AI trend output
materials: Printed trend data showing apparent "improvement" (PDF at landing page)
AI-generated improvement claims for the same data

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive printed trend data and AI-generated improvement claims. Read both carefully.

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams determine which improvement claims are genuine and which are artifacts of how the data was sliced (small sample, regression to mean, selective time window).

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written verdict: which improvement claims would survive Long Beach-grade scrutiny? Defend with evidence from the data.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verdict cites at least two genuine improvements and two artifacts, with the statistical reasoning behind each judgment.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

— Freyja Labs (working with Fresno Unified)

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Field to Table to Data: How AI Models the Food System" (grades 6-8, science/agriculture). Students use micro:bit sensors to measure soil and environmental conditions, then evaluate AI-generated agricultural recommendations. Embedded AI literacy: verification protocol applied to AI predictions about the system that feeds the Central Valley. Tailored to Fresno's agricultural identity.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI agricultural recommendations are guidance. After: students collect soil and environmental data themselves, evaluate AI predictions against the system that feeds the Central Valley, and learn that local knowledge is data the model needs and rarely has.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Field notes vs. algorithm
unplugged.title: Field Notes vs. Algorithms: Whose Knowledge Counts?
unplugged.overview: Teams compare printed agricultural recommendations: one from a local farmer with 30 years of experience, one from an AI model trained on national data. Students investigate when local knowledge outperforms large-scale models. Tailored to the Central Valley.
content_objectives: Compare two sources of agricultural recommendations
Identify the source of each divergence
Construct an evidence-based recommendation about appropriate use
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify scale-model blindspots in agricultural AI
Position local expert knowledge as authoritative
Articulate when each kind of knowledge applies
materials: Printed agricultural recommendations from a 30-year local farmer (PDF at landing page)
AI-generated agricultural recommendations trained on national data (same scenarios)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ read both sets of recommendations side by side. Note where they agree and where they diverge.

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams trace each divergence: does the local knowledge include something the AI lacks (microclimate, water-table specifics, crop history), or vice versa?

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a critique: in which growing decisions does local knowledge outperform large-scale AI models, and when is each appropriate? Connect to Central Valley realities.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Critique cites at least three specific divergences and ties each to a context where local or large-scale knowledge wins.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Fresno Unified)

Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information

unplugged.district_framing: Field Notes vs. Algorithms: Whose Knowledge Counts? — Teams compare printed agricultural recommendations: one from a local farmer with 30 years of experience, one from an AI model trained on national data. Students investigate when local knowledge outperforms large-scale models. Tailored to the Central Valley.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, "whose knowledge counts" is a discussion. After: students compare printed advice from a 30-year local farmer with AI recommendations trained on national data — and develop language for when local knowledge outperforms scale.

93721-fusd.districtNeeds: California is shaping what AI literacy means across core subjects through AB 2876 — math, science, and history-social science frameworks will all need to reflect AI thinking. For districts, this is a pedagogical design challenge: how do teachers integrate AI literacy into their existing practice without adding a new prep? The answer lies in structured approaches to lesson design that treat AI as a context for critical thinking rather than a tool to master. The CRAFT framework and the Check the Machine verification protocol are built for this — they help teachers design instruction where students learn to question, verify, and own their work when AI is part of the process.
93721-fusd.contextNarrative: Fresno Unified is the heart of the Central Valley — the 4th-largest district in California, serving an agricultural community where many students are the first in their families to pursue college. AI literacy is an equity tool here. The students who can verify, critique, and own their work in conversation with AI are the students who shape the technology rather than being shaped by it. AB 2876 is making that capacity statewide policy. We co-design with Fresno teachers — instruction that respects what students bring and prepares them for the conversations they'll be in for the next 50 years.

95624-egusd.connectionPoint: Diverse suburban Sacramento district. Rapid growth + changing demographics. Need for coherent framework amid change.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.badge: micro:bit + AI · Measuring change in a growing community
tech.title: New Neighbors, New Data: Understanding Change
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Elk Grove USD students in Elk Grove.
content_objectives: Measure environmental data
Identify recent community changes from observation
Compare AI pattern detection with student observation of change
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify where AI lags behind community change
Apply structured verification practice to AI pattern claims
Articulate when student observation outperforms AI pattern detection
materials: micro:bit with environmental sensors (included in kit)

procedure.title: Measure
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ collect environmental data on campus and document conditions that have changed recently (new building, new student arrivals, new traffic pattern).

procedure.title: Analyze
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to identify patterns in the data. Compare patterns the AI finds with the changes students have witnessed.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Class evaluates whether AI pattern detection handles change as well as students who live with the community shifts every day.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final evaluation cites at least one change students observed and one AI pattern claim, with a defensible judgment about which captured the reality better.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Elk Grove USD)

tech.badge: micro:bit + AI · Build a classifier from your own data
tech.title: How AI Learns: Building a Classifier From Your Data
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for San Jose USD students in San Jose.
content_objectives: Design a binary classification problem
Collect labeled training data
Train, test, and analyze a small classifier
ai_literacy_objectives: Understand AI from the engineering side
Identify how training data shape classifier behavior
Apply structured verification practice to a system the team built
materials: micro:bit with multiple sensor types (included in kit)
Browser-based ML classifier framework (link at landing page)

procedure.title: Train
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ design a binary classification problem (e.g., "indoor vs. outdoor", "morning vs. afternoon"), collect labeled training data with micro:bit sensors, and train a small classifier in the browser framework.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams test the classifier on new data. Document accuracy, identify systematic failures, and trace each failure to a training-data limitation.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to a system the team built. Class produces engineering-side insights about how AI systems learn and where they break — directly relevant to a Silicon Valley community.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final analysis names the classification problem, accuracy results, and at least two systematic failures traced to training data.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with San Jose USD)
