Students build a simple machine-learning classifier using micro:bit sensor data, then evaluate its accuracy. Embedded AI literacy: students see AI from the engineering side — how systems learn, where they break, and what the humans building them need to know. Designed for Silicon Valley — where understanding AI from the inside is a community expectation.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, building an ML classifier is a project. After: students see AI from the engineering side — how systems learn, where they break, what the humans building them need to know — exactly the depth a Silicon Valley community has every right to expect.

unplugged.badge: No screens · AI screener vs. human interviewer
unplugged.title: The Job Interview: AI Screener vs. Human Interviewer
unplugged.overview: Teams role-play both sides: an AI resume screener and a human interviewer. They discover what each approach reveals and conceals about a candidate. Designed for Silicon Valley, where many parents have been on both sides of this process.
content_objectives: Role-play distinct evaluation approaches
Compare what different methods reveal and conceal
Construct an evidence-based hiring recommendation
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify what AI screening captures and what it cannot
Articulate the comparative strengths of AI screening and human interviewing
Construct a role for each in fair hiring
materials: Printed fictional candidate resumes (PDF at landing page)
AI screener output for each candidate

procedure.title: Screen
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ split into two roles: an AI screener (must use only the resume data) and a human interviewer (designs open-ended questions). Document each role's reasoning.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams compare what each role surfaces. Document what an AI screener catches and misses; what a human interviewer catches and misses.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a position: how should hiring combine the two? Connect to Silicon Valley parents who have been on both sides.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Position cites specific candidate cases and names what each method revealed or concealed.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with San Jose USD)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: The Job Interview: AI Screener vs. Human Interviewer — Teams role-play both sides: an AI resume screener and a human interviewer. They discover what each approach reveals and conceals about a candidate. Designed for Silicon Valley, where many parents have been on both sides of this process.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, the AI vs. human-interviewer debate is conceptual. After: students role-play both sides — AI screener and human interviewer — and discover what each approach reveals and conceals. Many parents have been on both sides; the learning has somewhere to land.

95126-sjusd.districtNeeds: California is shaping what AI literacy means across core subjects through AB 2876 — math, science, and history-social science frameworks will all need to reflect AI thinking. For districts, this is a pedagogical design challenge: how do teachers integrate AI literacy into their existing practice without adding a new prep? The answer lies in structured approaches to lesson design that treat AI as a context for critical thinking rather than a tool to master. The CRAFT framework and the Check the Machine verification protocol are built for this — they help teachers design instruction where students learn to question, verify, and own their work when AI is part of the process.
95126-sjusd.contextNarrative: San Jose Unified is in the heart of Silicon Valley, where parents work in AI, ML, and semiconductor design. AI literacy is a community baseline expectation, not a novelty — and that creates a specific bar for the work. PD that treats AI as new ground will not land. AB 2876 is shaping the statewide policy frame, but for San Jose the harder question is pedagogical sophistication: how do teachers design instruction that pushes students past the consumer-level familiarity they already have, into rigorous evaluation of what tools can and cannot do? We design with that bar in mind — custom-built for San Jose teachers and students who arrive with home contexts most districts cannot assume.

80203-dps.connectionPoint: Most diverse student population in CO. School choice / portfolio model culture. Educator empowerment ethos.

engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. procedure.title: Choose

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Each team reflects: was AI a tool we used or a tool that used us? Class produces a shared list of practices that keep students in the driver seat — directly aligned with DPS's educator-empowerment culture.

Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Denver Public Schools)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: Choose Your Own Investigation: The Question Is the Point — Teams choose a community question they care about, design an investigation plan using only classroom materials, and predict how an AI would approach the same question differently. Students discover that the quality of the question determines the quality of the answer. Aligned with DPS's culture of student agency.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, "ask good questions" is a writing skill. After: students choose a community question they care about, design an investigation plan, and discover that the quality of the question determines the quality of any answer — AI included.

80203-dps.policyLandscape: CDE expanded CS standards (2024); CEI AI Roadmap
80203-dps.districtNeeds: Colorado's expanded CS standards (2024) include AI, cybersecurity, and digital citizenship strands, and the CEI Roadmap for AI in K-12 provides a thoughtful policy template. The ElevateAI cohort districts are demonstrating what phased integration looks like. For districts at earlier stages, the question is how to begin building teacher capacity in a way that creates lasting practice rather than one-off exposure. The CRAFT framework provides that foundation — a structured approach to lesson design that helps teachers develop computational thinking, AI literacy, and cybersecurity awareness through building and testing, with materials they continue to use and adapt.
80203-dps.contextNarrative: Denver Public Schools serves the most diverse student population in Colorado, with a school choice / portfolio model culture and an explicit educator empowerment ethos. PD here works when it respects teacher judgment, not when it scripts it. Colorado's expanded CS standards (2024) include AI, cybersecurity, and digital citizenship strands, and the CEI AI Roadmap provides a thoughtful policy template. The work ahead is helping teachers translate that policy frame into lasting classroom practice. We design with DPS teachers — custom-built foundations they direct, in the empowerment culture the district has organized around.

80401-jeffco.connectionPoint: Largest suburban CO district. Andrew Gitner leading AI assessment work. Already thinking beyond surface — assessment is the hard question.

tech.title: The Assessment Question: Original Thinking When AI Is Available
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Jefferson County (Jeffco) students in Golden.
content_objectives: Document reasoning during an investigation, not just results
Compare reasoning traces with AI output
Identify what student reasoning AI cannot replicate
ai_literacy_objectives: Distinguish AI-replicable work from work requiring student reasoning
Apply structured verification practice to assessment design
Articulate what makes an assessment AI-resistant

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ complete a micro:bit-based investigation. Document not just the result but the reasoning at each decision point.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to perform the same investigation/analysis. Compare AI output with the team's reasoning trace; identify what AI cannot replicate.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. The lesson itself becomes a model: what does an assessment look like that requires student reasoning even when AI is in the room? Connect directly to Andrew Gitner's assessment work.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: The artifact IS the assessment model — submitted reasoning trace cites moments only a student could have produced.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Jefferson County (Jeffco))

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

Embedded AI literacy: the lesson demonstrates what "AI-resistant" assessment looks like in practice. Tailored to Jeffco's AI assessment work under Andrew Gitner.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, "AI-resistant assessment" is a problem statement. After: students complete a micro:bit investigation, compare their reasoning with AI analysis, and the lesson itself models what AI-resistant assessment looks like — directly extending Andrew Gitner's work.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Class compares redesigns. Each team explains what their redesign demands of the student that AI cannot supply. Directly connected to Jeffco's assessment work.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol plus a precision audit. Class produces a list: when does AI improve measurement, and when does it introduce false precision?

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare

Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Douglas County SD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Precision: Why Good Data and Good Questions Matter More Than Good Tools" (grades 7-9, science/math). Students design precision measurement systems with micro:bit, then evaluate whether AI improves or degrades their accuracy. Embedded AI literacy: the discipline of questioning your tools. Designed for Douglas County's culture of high expectations.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI is the precision tool. After: students design precision measurement systems themselves, then evaluate whether AI improves or degrades their accuracy — the kind of disciplined questioning a high-expectations community recognizes as substance.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Estimation as a discipline
unplugged.title: Precision Without Tools: Estimation as a Discipline
unplugged.overview: Teams practice precision estimation challenges using only observation and reasoning. They compare their estimates with AI-generated predictions. Students discover that disciplined human observation often matches or exceeds algorithmic precision for local, specific questions.
content_objectives: Apply structured estimation techniques
Document reasoning behind an estimate
Compare estimation approaches and identify strengths
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify cases where local observation beats algorithmic estimation
Apply disciplined skepticism to AI precision claims
Articulate the value of human observation in measurement
materials: Printed estimation challenge prompts (PDF at landing page)
AI-generated estimates for the same prompts

procedure.title: Estimate
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ tackle estimation challenges (how many windows in this building? how many leaves on a tree?) using only observation and reasoning. Document the estimate and the reasoning.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams compare estimates with printed AI-generated estimates for the same prompts. Identify where each approach was stronger.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a verdict: when does disciplined human estimation outperform algorithmic precision? Connect to a community that values disciplined questioning.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verdict names at least one challenge where human estimation won and one where AI did, with evidence.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Douglas County SD)

unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, estimation is a math exercise. After: students practice precision estimation with only observation and reasoning, compare with AI predictions, and discover that disciplined human observation often matches algorithmic precision for local, specific questions.

80104-dcsd-co.districtNeeds: Colorado's expanded CS standards (2024) include AI, cybersecurity, and digital citizenship strands, and the CEI Roadmap for AI in K-12 provides a thoughtful policy template. The ElevateAI cohort districts are demonstrating what phased integration looks like. For districts at earlier stages, the question is how to begin building teacher capacity in a way that creates lasting practice rather than one-off exposure. The CRAFT framework provides that foundation — a structured approach to lesson design that helps teachers develop computational thinking, AI literacy, and cybersecurity awareness through building and testing, with materials they continue to use and adapt.
80104-dcsd-co.contextNarrative: Douglas County is among Colorado's highest-performing and most affluent districts, with a community that expects academic intensity and forward preparation. Professional respect is the currency that opens doors here — PD that doesn't treat teachers as professionals does not get a second invitation. Colorado's expanded CS standards (2024) include AI, cybersecurity, and digital citizenship strands. We design with Douglas County teachers — substantive, custom-built, materials your staff own and adapt long after the workshop ends.

80111-ccsd.connectionPoint: Southeast Denver metro tech/aerospace corridor. College readiness reputation. Expects depth, not exposure.
80111-ccsd.standardsFramework: Colorado Academic Standards (CAS). CS: CDE CS Standards (2024, includes AI/cybersecurity/digital citizenship strands). Science: CAS Science. Math: CAS Math. AI: align to CEI AI Roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: College Ready, AI Ready: Critical Thinking for Intelligent Systems
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Cherry Creek SD students in Greenwood Village.
content_objectives: Investigate a multidisciplinary problem
Construct a college-level evidence-based argument
Identify systematic limitations across a problem domain
ai_literacy_objectives: Apply college-level critical thinking to AI output

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ choose a multidisciplinary problem (climate, equity, public health) and use micro:bit data plus AI analysis to investigate it.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team writes a college-level evidence-based argument about the limits of AI for this problem. Cite specific moments where AI fell short.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Class shares arguments and identifies common failure modes — the kind of college-prep critical thinking Cherry Creek expects.

tech.title: Whose Voice? AI and Multilingual Data
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Aurora Public Schools students in Aurora.
content_objectives: Anchor analysis with shared objective data
Compare AI behavior across multiple languages
Position multilingual fluency as analytical authority
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify AI bias through linguistic diversity
Apply a structured verification practice with multilingual evidence
Articulate what English-centric AI misses
materials: micro:bit with sensors (included in kit) — used as data anchor

procedure.title: Anchor
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ collect a small micro:bit dataset to provide an objective anchor. Document data and observations.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool in multiple community languages — students use their own home languages. Compare AI performance differences using the micro:bit data as the shared reference.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Each team produces evidence about AI bias surfaced by Aurora's 130+ languages — multilingual students become the experts.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Evidence cites at least one specific AI behavior difference per language and what it reveals about training-data assumptions.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Aurora Public Schools)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

Students test AI tools in multiple languages and evaluate performance differences. Micro:bit data provides an anchor of objective measurement. Embedded AI literacy: AI bias through the lens of linguistic diversity. Tailored to Aurora's 130+ languages.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI bias is a topic. After: students test AI in multiple languages and evaluate performance differences — using their own multilingual community as the dataset that reveals the bias. Aurora's 130+ languages aren't the variable; they're the experiment.

unplugged.badge: No screens · 130 languages testing AI
unplugged.title: The Language Test: AI Across 130 Voices
unplugged.overview: Teams examine printed AI translations and outputs in multiple languages. Students with multilingual backgrounds evaluate accuracy and identify where AI mishandles their home language. Students become the experts. Tailored to Aurora's linguistic diversity.
content_objectives: Evaluate written output in a language the student knows well
Document linguistic and cultural mishandling
Construct a structured multilingual evidence report
ai_literacy_objectives: Position multilingual fluency as authoritative AI evaluation evidence
Identify systematic AI weaknesses by language
Articulate the cost of English-centric AI training
materials: Printed AI translations and outputs in multiple community languages (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ work in linguistic groups. Each group reviews printed AI outputs in their home language and annotates where the AI handled the language well or poorly.

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams identify cultural and linguistic patterns that AI mishandles — students with multilingual backgrounds become the authoritative experts.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written report on AI performance in their language. Class compiles a multilingual AI-bias map of Aurora's communities.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Multilingual report names at least three specific mishandlings per language and the cultural context required to catch each.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

procedure.title: Role-play
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ role-play workplace scenarios. The AI system recommends one action; the employee's judgment suggests another. Document the reasoning on both sides.

check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Override framework cites at least three scenario categories and the criteria for each.

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com

80501-svvsd.districtNeeds: Colorado's expanded CS standards (2024) include AI, cybersecurity, and digital citizenship strands, and the CEI Roadmap for AI in K-12 provides a thoughtful policy template. The ElevateAI cohort districts are demonstrating what phased integration looks like. For districts at earlier stages, the question is how to begin building teacher capacity in a way that creates lasting practice rather than one-off exposure. The CRAFT framework provides that foundation — a structured approach to lesson design that helps teachers develop computational thinking, AI literacy, and cybersecurity awareness through building and testing, with materials they continue to use and adapt.
80501-svvsd.contextNarrative: St. Vrain Valley runs an Innovation Center model with strong industry partnerships and a track record of connecting classroom learning to workforce experiences. PD here should deepen what's already in motion, not introduce concepts the district has been operationalizing for years. Colorado's expanded CS standards (2024) and the CEI AI Roadmap give the next layer of policy structure. We co-design with St. Vrain — custom-built to extend the Innovation Center's classroom-to-industry connection, with practices students carry into internships and beyond.
