teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Prince George's County PS)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Every School, Every Student: How AI Can Support (or Undermine) Equity" (grades 6-8, science/social studies). Students examine how AI tools perform differently in different school contexts — and what that means for equitable access. Embedded AI literacy: AI equity evaluated through direct student experience.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI works the same regardless of school. After: students examine how AI tools perform differently in different school contexts — and produce evidence of where AI supports equity and where it undermines it. Equity work, classroom-level.

unplugged.badge: No screens · The equity audit
unplugged.title: Every School Deserves This: The Equity Audit
unplugged.overview: Teams examine how a fictional AI resource-allocation system distributes materials across schools with different demographics. They identify bias and propose corrections. Connected to PG County's equity mission.
content_objectives: Read system output for distributional patterns
Apply equity criteria to algorithmic decisions
Construct corrective recommendations
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify equity bias in algorithmic resource allocation
Apply audit standards to AI systems
Articulate corrective interventions
materials: Printed AI resource-allocation system output (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ examine the printed AI system's output for resource allocation across schools with different demographics. Document the patterns.

procedure.title: Audit
procedure.description: Teams apply equity criteria from the audit worksheet. Identify bias patterns and propose specific corrections.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces an audit report with documented bias and proposed corrections. Connected directly to PGCPS's equity mission.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Audit report cites at least two specific bias patterns and proposes a concrete correction for each.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Prince George's County PS)

unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, an AI resource-allocation system is a black box. After: students examine how a fictional system distributes materials across schools with different demographics, identify the bias, and propose corrections — exactly the equity audit PGCPS's mission demands.

20772-pgcps.policyLandscape: CS-for-all in place; SB 980 adds AI/cybersecurity
20772-pgcps.districtNeeds: Maryland's CS-for-all requirement is in place, and SB 980 (2024) adds AI and cybersecurity to the standards. The MSDE AI Guidance (February 2026) and Classroom Companion provide the policy framework; districts now need to operationalize it — translating guidance into teacher practice. MCCE grants ($3K-$50K) can fund this work. The CRAFT framework and Check the Machine protocol provide the pedagogical structure teachers need to design instruction that integrates AI and cybersecurity concepts into their existing curriculum — and that holds up as the technology and policy landscape continues to evolve.
20772-pgcps.contextNarrative: Prince George's County is the 18th-largest district in the U.S., serving a community shaped by the federal government, Howard University, and the military corridor — under Superintendent House's leadership with explicit equity focus. The implication for PD is structural: the work has to land at every school, not just the well-resourced ones. Maryland's CS-for-all and SB 980's AI and cybersecurity additions create the policy expectation. We co-design with PGCPS — custom-built to reach every school, not just the well-resourced ones, with materials and practices that continue past the workshop.

21204-bcps.connectionPoint: Towson to Dundalk to Catonsville. Suburban, industrial, rural in one district. Framework-based PD provides coherence across geographic diversity.
21204-bcps.standardsFramework: Maryland College and Career Ready Standards (MCCRS). CS: MD CS Standards + SB 980 AI/cybersecurity additions. Science: MD NGSS. Math: MD CCSS-M. Align to MSDE AI Guidance Classroom Companion.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: Across the County: Data in Different Contexts
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Baltimore County PS students in Towson.
content_objectives: Collect and document context-paired data
Compare AI analysis across contexts
Identify limits of cross-context generalization
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify when AI analysis transfers between contexts
Apply structured verification practice to cross-context generalization
Articulate context-sensitivity criteria
materials: Building-context cards (urban, suburban, rural Baltimore County)

procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ collect data on campus and pair it with a building-context card describing a different Baltimore County setting.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to analyze their data using one context, then prompt it to apply the same analysis to the contrast context. Document differences.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Class produces evidence about whether AI analysis transfers across the urban-suburban-rural span of Baltimore County.

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality

facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Baltimore County PS)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: The Geography Test: Does This Apply Here? — Teams evaluate whether printed AI recommendations (designed for one community type) apply in their own school context. Students discover that context transfer is the hardest problem in AI. Connected to Baltimore County's Towson-to-Dundalk diversity.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, AI recommendations apply universally. After: students evaluate whether printed AI recommendations designed for one community type apply in their own school — and discover that context transfer is the hardest problem AI faces.

21204-bcps.policyLandscape: CS-for-all in place; SB 980 adds AI/cybersecurity
21204-bcps.districtNeeds: Maryland's CS-for-all requirement is in place, and SB 980 (2024) adds AI and cybersecurity to the standards. The MSDE AI Guidance (February 2026) and Classroom Companion provide the policy framework; districts now need to operationalize it — translating guidance into teacher practice. MCCE grants ($3K-$50K) can fund this work. The CRAFT framework and Check the Machine protocol provide the pedagogical structure teachers need to design instruction that integrates AI and cybersecurity concepts into their existing curriculum — and that holds up as the technology and policy landscape continues to evolve.
21204-bcps.contextNarrative: Baltimore County stretches from Towson through Dundalk to Catonsville — suburban, industrial, and rural inside a single district. Framework-based PD provides the coherence that geographic diversity otherwise scatters. Maryland's CS-for-all is in place, and SB 980 (2024) adds AI and cybersecurity to the standards. We co-design with Baltimore County teachers — custom-built for the geographic diversity, with shared design language that doesn't force uniformity where it doesn't fit.

21401-aacps.connectionPoint: Naval Academy, Fort Meade, NSA, U.S. Cyber Command. Cybersecurity education is the family business. 15 years of cybersecurity ed research resonates here uniquely.
21401-aacps.standardsFramework: Maryland College and Career Ready Standards (MCCRS). CS: MD CS Standards + SB 980 AI/cybersecurity additions. Science: MD NGSS. Math: MD CCSS-M. Align to MSDE AI Guidance Classroom Companion.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: Signals and Noise: Cybersecurity Thinking for AI
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Anne Arundel County PS students in Annapolis.
content_objectives: Build a working micro:bit communication system
Test the system under controlled disruption
Apply cybersecurity verification principles
ai_literacy_objectives: Apply cybersecurity verification thinking to AI output
Identify limits of AI verification under noise
Articulate the connection between cybersecurity and AI literacy
materials: micro:bit with radio capability (included in kit) — paired devices

procedure.title: Build
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ build a micro:bit communication system using radio. Send and receive messages between paired devices and document baseline accuracy.

procedure.title: Disrupt
procedure.description: Teams introduce noise (interference, partial transmission). Run AI analysis to verify the integrity of received messages. Document where AI catches corruption and where it fails.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply cybersecurity verification principles plus the verification protocol to AI output verification. Class produces a Cyber Command-grade list of AI verification techniques and their limits.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final list cites at least one AI verification technique that worked, one that failed, and the noise condition for each.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Anne Arundel County PS)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Signals and Noise: How Cybersecurity Thinking Applies to AI Verification" (grades 7-10, science/technology). Students build a micro:bit communication system, introduce noise, then apply cybersecurity principles to AI output verification. Embedded AI literacy: cybersecurity thinking as a foundation for AI critical evaluation. Designed for the NSA/Cyber Command community.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI verification and cybersecurity verification are different fields. After: students build a micro:bit communication system, introduce noise, and apply cybersecurity principles to AI verification — the connection a Naval Academy / NSA / Cyber Command community grasps immediately.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Signals and noise verification game
unplugged.title: Signals and Noise: The Cybersecurity Verification Game
unplugged.overview: Teams play a paper-based message-verification game where they must distinguish authentic messages from spoofed ones using only printed evidence and reasoning. The same verification logic applies to AI outputs. Designed for the NSA/Cyber Command community.
content_objectives: Apply structured verification reasoning
Distinguish authentic from spoofed messages
Identify verification techniques
ai_literacy_objectives: Apply cybersecurity verification reasoning to AI outputs
Identify which techniques transfer between domains
Articulate the parallel between message and AI verification
materials: Printed message-verification game cards (PDF at landing page) — authentic and spoofed messages

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ receive printed message cards — some authentic, some spoofed. Document initial impressions of each.

procedure.title: Verify
procedure.description: Teams apply cybersecurity verification principles using only printed evidence and reasoning. Document why each judgment was reached.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a verdict on each message and explains the reasoning. Class identifies which verification techniques transfer directly to evaluating AI outputs.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verdict cites at least three messages, the reasoning, and the cybersecurity principle applied.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

procedure.title: Collect

procedure.title: Analyze
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to characterize the neighborhood from the data. Document where AI characterization matches community knowledge and where it falls short.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Each team produces evidence positioning community expertise as a check on AI characterization — aligned with Baltimore's self-determination tradition.

The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

— Freyja Labs (working with Baltimore City PS)

unplugged.title: Our Block Knows: Community Knowledge vs. Algorithmic Knowledge
unplugged.overview: Teams compare printed AI-generated descriptions of Baltimore neighborhoods with their own lived experience. Students become the authoritative source. Connected to Baltimore's culture of community expertise and self-determination.
content_objectives: Read AI descriptions critically
Identify factual, framing, and absence issues
Position community knowledge as authoritative
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify AI failure modes for community description
Apply community knowledge as verification

materials: Printed AI-generated descriptions of Baltimore neighborhoods (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ read printed AI descriptions of Baltimore neighborhoods they know. Annotate where the descriptions feel accurate and where they feel off.

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Teams document specifically what the AI got wrong — facts, framing, or absence — drawing on lived community knowledge.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team produces a written verdict positioning students as the authoritative source on their own neighborhoods. Aligned with Baltimore's tradition of community self-determination.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Verdict cites at least three specific AI errors per neighborhood and pairs each with community knowledge.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

Dear families,

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com

21202-bcpss.policyLandscape: CS-for-all in place; SB 980 adds AI/cybersecurity
21202-bcpss.districtNeeds: Maryland's CS-for-all requirement is in place, and SB 980 (2024) adds AI and cybersecurity to the standards. The MSDE AI Guidance (February 2026) and Classroom Companion provide the policy framework; districts now need to operationalize it — translating guidance into teacher practice. MCCE grants ($3K-$50K) can fund this work. The CRAFT framework and Check the Machine protocol provide the pedagogical structure teachers need to design instruction that integrates AI and cybersecurity concepts into their existing curriculum — and that holds up as the technology and policy landscape continues to evolve.
21202-bcpss.contextNarrative: Baltimore City Public Schools is shaped by Johns Hopkins, the University of Maryland, and a thriving arts scene — a community-driven education culture with deep traditions of self-determination. PD that builds from within aligns with that culture; PD imposed from outside does not. Maryland's CS-for-all is in place, and SB 980 (2024) adds AI and cybersecurity to the standards. We co-design with Baltimore City teachers — capacity-building that grows from within, in the tradition of community self-determination the district has built around.

21042-hcpss.connectionPoint: A+ Niche. Baltimore-Washington corridor. Defense, tech, biomedical sectors. Among the most educated communities in the nation. Expects intellectual rigor.
21042-hcpss.standardsFramework: Maryland College and Career Ready Standards (MCCRS). CS: MD CS Standards + SB 980 AI/cybersecurity additions. Science: MD NGSS. Math: MD CCSS-M. Align to MSDE AI Guidance Classroom Companion.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: The Research Question: Scientists vs. AI Analysts
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Howard County PS students in Ellicott City.
content_objectives: Design a research methodology with explicit reasoning
Compare student methodology with AI methodology
Articulate the difference between research and querying
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify what AI inquiry methodology lacks
Apply structured verification practice to AI methodology
Articulate research-grade differences between human and AI inquiry

procedure.title: Design
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ design a research investigation using micro:bit data. Document the research question, methodology, and predicted findings.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams query an AI tool to perform the same investigation. Document the AI's methodology — what it asked, what it analyzed, what it concluded.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol. Class compares: what is the difference between asking a research question and querying a model? Designed for one of the most educated communities in the nation.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Methodology comparison cites at least three specific differences between team and AI approaches.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com

tech.badge: micro:bit + AI · Build a classifier from the inside
tech.title: Your Parents Build This: AI From the Inside
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Seattle Public Schools students in Seattle.
content_objectives: Design a binary classification task
Train and test a small classifier
Identify systematic failure patterns
ai_literacy_objectives: Understand AI from the engineering side
Identify how training-data choices shape behavior
Apply structured verification practice to a system the team built

procedure.title: Train
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ design a binary classification problem and train a small classifier using micro:bit sensor data. Document training-data choices.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams test the classifier on new data. Identify where it succeeds and where it fails systematically.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply structured verification practice to a system the team built. Class produces engineering-side insights — relevant to a community where families build these systems.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we used or generated?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses, instruments, or knowledge?

check_the_machine.label: Check yourself
check_the_machine.prompt: What might we have missed? What would we want a second opinion on?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final analysis names training-data choices, test results, and at least two systematic failures linked to training-data limitations.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The verification protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The verification protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply structured verification practice to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol the verification protocol. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Seattle Public Schools)

Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Your Parents Build This: Understanding AI From the Inside" (grades 7-10, science/technology). Students build a simple AI classifier using micro:bit sensor data, then evaluate its decisions — experiencing the engineering side of AI. Embedded AI literacy: understanding AI as a system humans design, not a system that designs itself.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI is what their parents build at Amazon, Microsoft, or Boeing. After: students build a simple AI classifier themselves and evaluate its decisions — experiencing the engineering side of AI as a system humans design, not a system that designs itself.

unplugged.badge: No screens · Paper prototype of training data bias
unplugged.title: Build the Bias: A Paper Prototype of How Training Data Shapes AI
unplugged.overview: Teams create a simple paper-based "AI system" by writing rules from a limited dataset, then test it on new data. They discover how training data bias emerges from the ground up. Designed for Seattle, where many families build these systems for a living.
content_objectives: Construct classification rules from training data
Test rules against new data
Trace failures to training-data limitations
ai_literacy_objectives: Identify how training data shapes AI behavior
Distinguish bias from accuracy
Articulate the role of training-data choices in real AI systems
materials: Printed limited training datasets (PDF at landing page)

procedure.title: Build
procedure.description: Teams of 3+ create a paper "AI system" by writing classification rules from a limited printed training dataset.

procedure.title: Test
procedure.description: Teams test their rules against new data cards. Document where the rules succeed and where they produce wrong or unfair results.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Each team identifies how training-data limitations produced specific biases. Class compiles patterns relevant to a community whose families build production AI systems.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: Each team produces a one-page artifact: their findings, the AI output they evaluated, and a written verdict on when this kind of AI work is worth trusting.
assessment.criteria: Final analysis cites at least two specific bias patterns and the training-data limitation that produced each.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Verification protocol — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part verification protocol: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Seattle Public Schools)

Describe tradeoffs between public and private information

unplugged.district_framing: Build the Bias: A Paper Prototype of How Training Data Shapes AI — Teams create a simple paper-based "AI system" by writing rules from a limited dataset, then test it on new data. They discover how training data bias emerges from the ground up. Designed for Seattle, where many families build these systems for a living.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, AI bias is something engineers debug. After: students build a paper "AI system" by writing rules from a limited dataset, test it on new data, and discover how training data bias emerges from the ground up — the same pattern their families work to fix.

98134-sps.districtNeeds: Washington's Human-Centered AI Guidance (now in its third edition) and the H-AI-H framework (Human inquiry, AI production, Human reflection) set a strong intellectual foundation. Districts are working toward CS competency requirements, and OSPI CS & Education Grants support PD. The pedagogical question is how teachers operationalize 'human-centered AI' in daily instruction — what does it look like when a 5th grader or a 10th grader uses AI as part of learning and is expected to reflect critically on the output? The CRAFT framework and Check the Machine protocol align directly with the H-AI-H approach and give teachers concrete practices to build on.
98134-sps.contextNarrative: Seattle Public Schools serves the city where Amazon, Microsoft, and Boeing share an address — 109 schools where AI literacy is current reality, not future concept, under Superintendent Jones's leadership. Washington's Human-Centered AI Guidance (now in its third edition) and the H-AI-H framework — Human inquiry, AI production, Human reflection — give the state's pedagogical direction. We co-design with Seattle teachers — custom-built to operationalize Washington's Human-Centered AI framework in classrooms across all 109 schools.

98052-lwsd.connectionPoint: Microsoft HQ is within district boundary (Redmond). Families work in AI and cloud computing. Challenge is pedagogical sophistication, not concept introduction.
98052-lwsd.standardsFramework: Washington State Learning Standards. CS: WA K-12 CS Standards. Science: WA NGSS. Math: WA CCSS-M. AI literacy: align to OSPI Human-Centered AI Guidance (H-AI-H framework).

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Apply the verification protocol plus an explainability audit. Class produces a Microsoft-corridor-grade insight: explainability is a design problem, not a simplification problem.
